meta.title: Mercedes E500 E550 Engine Replacement Cost UK — Compare Quotes
meta.description: Every Mercedes‑Benz E500 / E550 engine — M113, M273 and M278. W211, W212 and C207/A207 generations, real UK costs and reliability. Get free quotes from 25+ vetted Mercedes specialists today.

@graph.name: Mercedes‑Benz E500 / E550 Engine Era Map

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes‑Benz E500 / E550 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned engine typically costs £1,500–£8,500 supply only depending on generation: M113 (£1,500–£3,500), M273 (£2,500–£5,000), M278 (£4,000–£8,500). Fitting adds £900–£1,600. Balance shaft replacement on the M273 can cost £1,500–£4,000 if caught early. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your car.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes‑Benz E500 / E550 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on the generation. The M113 (2002–2006) is widely considered bulletproof — a robust 3‑valve V8. The M273 (2006–2011) has a notorious balance shaft failure on early cars (2006–2007). The M278 (2011–2016) is a twin‑turbo with timing chain tensioner and oil leak issues. Post‑2008 M273 cars are significantly more reliable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes‑Benz E500 / E550?

acceptedAnswer.text: M113 oil leaks (valve covers, oil cooler seals); M273 balance shaft / idler gear failure (early cars) and intake manifold flap failure; M278 timing chain tensioner rattle, cam magnet / cam sensor oil leaks, turbo coolant line failures, and conductor plate failure in the 722.9 transmission.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes‑Benz E500 / E550 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. Against a well‑kept W212 E550 worth £6,000–£12,000, a £4,000–£8,500 recon is a significant investment — assess carefully. For an M113‑powered W211 E500 worth £3,000–£7,000, a £3,500 recon is more marginal. However, well‑maintained V8 E‑Classes are increasingly collectable.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes‑Benz E500 / E550 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The E500 uses M113 (W211 2002–2006) or M273 (W211 2006–2009, W212 2009–2011). The E550 uses M273 (W211 2006–2009, W212 2009–2011) or M278 (W212 2011–2016, C207/A207 2011–2017). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm it for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the M273's balance shaft, or does it need a new engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A balance shaft / idler gear replacement (£1,500–£4,000) is far cheaper than a catastrophic engine failure (£2,500–£5,000 engine replacement). Check your engine serial number — if it's in the affected range, book an inspection immediately. Post‑2008 cars are not affected.

eraMap.title: The E500 / E550 THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: The "bulletproof" M113 — robust 3‑valve V8 with 722.6 5‑speed; oil leaks and crankcase vent issues are the main watchpoints.
The 4‑valve M273 — 385 bhp; balance shaft/idler gear failure (pre‑2008) and intake manifold flaps are the defining weaknesses.
Late M273 — balance shaft issue largely resolved, but oil leaks and intake manifold problems remain.
The twin‑turbo M278 — 402 bhp; timing chain tensioner, cam magnet oil leaks, and coolant line failures are the main watchpoints.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes-Benz E500 / E550 Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an E500 / E550 engine typically takes 12–16 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). Balance shaft replacement on the M273 is an engine‑out job, significantly increasing labour costs.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Mercedes-Benz E500 / E550 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.problem: M113 oil leaks (valve covers, oil cooler)
rows.whenItMakesSense: Straightforward gasket replacement; catch it early

rows.problem: M273 balance shaft / idler gear failure
rows.whenItMakesSense: If caught early; engine‑out job but cheaper than replacement
Replace manifold or fit repair kit; catch it before cat damage
TSB LI05.1o-P-056435 covers this; catch it before chain jumps

rows.problem: M278 cam magnet / sensor oil leaks
rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catch it before oil reaches the ECU
Replace coolant lines and hoses — common failure
Replace conductor plate and valve body; common around 60,000 miles

buyingChecks.title: Buying This E500 / E550? Check These First
buyIf: * W211 E500 with the M113 (2002–2006) — the "bulletproof" V8
* M278 with documented timing chain tensioner replacement (TSB work completed)
* Full Mercedes service history — all V8s are sensitive to oil quality
avoidIf: * 2006–2007 M273 with no balance shaft history — check the engine serial number immediately
* M278 with cold‑start rattle and no timing chain tensioner history
* Oil in camshaft sensor connectors — potential ECU damage risk
* Poor service history on any generation — V8s are unforgiving of neglect
* 2008–2009 E500/E550 with the M273 — balance shaft issues should be resolved by 2008

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The E500 / E550 badge spans three V8 families — and the reliability varies dramatically. The M113 (2002–2006) is widely considered bulletproof. The M273 (2006–2011) has a notorious balance shaft failure on early cars. The M278 (2011–2016) is a twin‑turbo marvel with timing chain tensioner and oil leak issues. Buy the M113 if you want bulletproof reliability; buy a post‑2008 M273 or a well‑maintained M278 if you want more power. If you're replacing, a reconditioned unit with a 12‑month warranty is the sweet spot.

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes‑Benz E500 / E550 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Engine Serial Number — For M273, check if it's in the affected range for balance shaft failure (2006–2007)
* Cold Start — Listen for rattling noise on startup (M273 balance shaft or M278 timing chain tensioner)
* Service History — Including which engine era (M113 for 2002–2006 W211, M273 for 2006–2011, M278 for 2011–2016) — see the Era Map above
* Oil Leaks — Check for oil spots under the car (M113 valve covers, M278 cam sensors)
* Cam Sensor Connectors — Disconnect and check for oil — oil in the plug could mean a new ECU (M278)
* Intake Manifold — Any loss of power or hesitation? Intake manifold flap failure (M273/M278)
* Transmission — Any harsh shifting? 722.9 conductor plate failure is common
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved timing, intake, or emissions issues?
